skill.name: session-memory
skill.description: Maintains local project memory across sessions by reading and updating session logs, decisions, reusable patterns, and project context files.
skill.summary: Resume project work with local session history and documented decisions.
skill.author: Chachamaru127

security_audit.summary: Static command-execution, weak-crypto, reconnaissance, and obfuscation findings were false positives caused by Markdown backticks, plain text, and examples in SKILL.md. The meaningful risk is local persistence: the skill directs agents to read and write memory files that may contain sensitive project history if users record it.

medium_findings.title: Persistent Local Memory May Store Sensitive Context
medium_findings.description: The skill declares file write tools and directs agents to store session logs, decisions, patterns, and context under .claude/memory/. This is legitimate for continuity, but it can preserve secrets, private implementation details, or sensitive customer data if users include them in session history.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The declared Read, Write, and Append tools and the documented .claude/memory workflow confirm local persistence. The privacy warning reduces malicious intent concerns, but it does not remove the data retention risk.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Backticks Reported as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged many Markdown backticks as Ruby or shell execution. The reviewed lines are prose, file paths, fenced examples, and sample output, with no executable Ruby code or shell command invocation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged content is visible Markdown documentation and examples, not a script. No shell execution syntax is present outside Markdown formatting.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography and Obfuscation Signals
low_findings.description: The high-risk weak cryptography and entropy alerts do not match the reviewed file. SKILL.md is readable Markdown with Japanese and English text, and no cryptographic algorithm, encoded payload, binary blob, or encrypted content was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Direct review shows frontmatter and natural-language documentation at the cited lines. The entropy signal is explained by multilingual text and Markdown structure rather than obfuscation.

low_findings.title: False Positive: System Reconnaissance Trigger Phrase
low_findings.description: The static system reconnaissance alert points to a user trigger phrase about prior work. It does not instruct the agent to inspect the host, enumerate system details, or collect environment information.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited text is an English trigger phrase for session history. No system inspection behavior is described at that location.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local File Persistence
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill uses filesystem tools and persistent memory files. Users should avoid storing credentials, private customer data, or sensitive implementation details in these logs.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Filesystem persistence is explicitly declared and documented. The risk depends on user behavior, but the storage behavior is clear.

content.value_statement: Teams lose time when agents forget prior decisions, project status, and unfinished tasks. This skill organizes local session memory so Claude, Codex, and Claude Code can continue work with clearer context.
actual_capabilities: Defines a .claude/memory/ structure for session logs, decisions, patterns, and context.
Guides agents to read prior session context before resuming project work.
Separates shared decision records from local session logs and state files.
Provides sample formats for Markdown logs and a JSON context file.
Reminds users not to record sensitive information in memory files.
limitations: Does not include executable automation or hook files in this package.
Depends on the agent and user to keep memory files accurate.
Can accumulate stale or sensitive information if not maintained.
Uses local files and does not synchronize memory across machines.

use_cases.title: Continue Development After a Break
use_cases.description: Review previous work, unfinished tasks, and project status before starting a new coding session.

use_cases.title: Preserve Team Decisions
use_cases.description: Record important technical choices and reusable project patterns in shared Markdown files.

use_cases.title: Create Clear Project Handoffs
use_cases.description: Summarize recent progress and next steps so another agent or teammate can continue safely.

prompt_templates.title: Show Previous Session
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner: inspect recent history before working.
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me what happened in the previous session and list the unfinished tasks.

prompt_templates.title: Summarize Project Context
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner to intermediate: rebuild project awareness.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the session memory files to summarize this project, recent decisions, and current phase.

prompt_templates.title: Update Memory After Work
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate: maintain continuity after completing tasks.
prompt_templates.prompt: Update the session memory with completed tasks, changed files, important decisions, and next steps. Do not include secrets.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Memory Quality
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced: clean and improve long-running project memory.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the session memory for stale entries, duplicate decisions, missing context, and sensitive information. Suggest a cleaner structure.

output_examples.input: What did we do last time?
output_examples.output: A concise recap of completed tasks, generated files, recent decisions, and unresolved follow-up work.
A session summary with completed work, important decisions, reusable patterns, and clear next steps.
A project status summary that includes stack, current phase, progress, and notable recent decisions.

best_practices: Keep shared decision and pattern files concise, indexed, and reviewed.
Exclude credentials, tokens, customer data, and private notes from memory files.
Periodically prune old session logs and move durable knowledge into decision records.
anti_patterns: Using session memory as a full transcript archive for every interaction.
Committing local session logs or context files without reviewing their contents.
Treating stale memory as authoritative when current repository files disagree.

faq.question: Does this skill store memory outside the project?
faq.answer: No evidence found for external storage. The documented design uses local files under .claude/memory/.

faq.question: Does it run commands or install software?
faq.answer: No. The reviewed skill file is documentation and declares Read, Write, and Append tools only.

faq.question: Can it work with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: What should be shared in version control?
faq.answer: The skill recommends sharing decisions and reusable patterns, while keeping session logs and local context private by default.

faq.question: What information should never be stored?
faq.answer: Do not store credentials, API keys, personal data, customer data, or private security details.

faq.question: Why is the security rating medium?
faq.answer: The skill is not malicious, but it can retain sensitive project history if users write unsafe content to local memory files.
